title_en: On Certainty and Reliance on Allah — Yaqin and Tawakkul
content_en:
Certainty (yaqin) and reliance on Allah (tawakkul) are treated by al-Nawawi as interconnected realities — yaqin is the firmness of conviction that makes tawakkul possible, and tawakkul is the practical expression of that certainty in the ordering of one's life. Together, they represent a radical reorientation of the human soul away from its habitual dependence on means, strategies, and created sources of security, toward exclusive reliance on the One who controls all means and all outcomes.

The primary text for this chapter is the famous hadith of the birds: 'If you rely on Allah as He should truly be relied upon, He would provide for you as He provides for the birds — they go out hungry in the morning and return full in the evening.' (Al-Tirmidhi, Ahmad, authenticated by al-Albani.) This narration is often misunderstood as recommending passivity — the birds, after all, do go out in search of food; they do not simply sit in their nests waiting for provisions to arrive. The lesson is not the abandonment of effort (which the Quran commands: 'And when the prayer is finished, then disperse through the land and seek of Allah's bounty' — Quran 62:10) but rather the proper orientation of the heart during effort: not trusting in the effort itself, but in the Allah who has made effort the means by which He provides.

Al-Nawawi includes the hadith: 'Know that if the entire nation were to gather together to benefit you, they would not benefit you except with something that Allah had already prescribed for you, and if they gathered together to harm you, they would not harm you except with something that Allah had already decreed against you.' (Al-Tirmidhi.) This narration is a complete theology of tawakkul in miniature: it acknowledges the role of created beings in the delivery of benefit and harm, while placing ultimate causation exclusively with Allah. The person who genuinely believes this is freed from the anxiety of seeking favor from the powerful and the fear of the malice of the hostile — because neither can ultimately change what Allah has decreed.

The chapter also includes the hadith about Umar ibn al-Khattab hearing the Prophet ﷺ say: 'If you relied on Allah with true reliance, He would provide for you just as He provides for the birds.' Umar's comment on this — 'People misunderstand tawakkul — they think it means abandoning all action. But tawakkul means taking all the necessary means and then placing your trust in Allah for the outcome' — reflects the classical Sunni position on this virtue. Tawakkul is not the absence of means-taking but the absence of dependence on those means — a fine but crucial distinction.

Al-Nawawi's vision of yaqin goes beyond intellectual certainty to a quality of unshakeable conviction that transforms behavior. The person of high yaqin does not waver in worship when results are not immediately visible; does not compromise their principles when social pressure mounts; and does not despair in hardship because their certainty that Allah is in control is not shaken by circumstances. This is the certainty that characterized the Companions in the most desperate moments of early Islam and is the quality that al-Nawawi seeks to cultivate in every reader of Riyadh al-Salihin.
